summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox that while most programmers don't read books, the best programming books remain invaluable. He criticizes the technical book publishing industry for producing mostly low-quality, bloated, or overly basic content that has been rightfully supplanted by online resources. However, he argues that truly great programming books are timeless because they explain 'why' rather than 'how' and transcend specific languages or platforms. The post concludes with a passionate defense of classic programming literature and a call for developers to read the foundational texts that reward repeated reading throughout a career.
key_points: The average software developer doesn't own or read books about programming, a trend that predates the internet
Most programming books fail because they're too long, too basic, or focus on quick-fix solutions rather than deep understanding
The internet has made most programming books obsolete for practical, language-specific information
Timeless programming books focus on 'why' rather than 'how' and don't need to be replaced every five years
Stack Overflow was designed to complement classic programming books, not replace them
money_quotes: The average software developer, for example, doesn't own a single book on the subject of his or her work, and hasn't ever read one.
The internet has rendered programming books obsolete. It's faster, more efficient, and just plain smarter to get your programming information online.
There seems to be an inverse relationship between the size of a programming book and its quality.
The monomaniacal focus on right now and the fastest, easiest possible way to do things leads beginners down the wrong path – or as I like to call it, 'PHP.'
Programming book pornography. The idea that having a pile of thick, important-looking programming books sitting on your shelf, largely unread, will somehow make you a better programmer.
If you feel compelled to clean house on your bookshelf every five years, trust me on this, you're buying the wrong programming books.
The best programming books are timeless. They transcend choice of language, IDE, or platform. They do not explain how, but why.
key_insight: While most programming books deserve their obsolescence, the timeless classics that explain 'why' rather than 'how' remain essential reading that distinguishes great developers from the rest.
